I see where you're coming from, but the IOCP Loop for windows and the
Selector Loop for unix are super-classes because they need to be.

It seems like you're saying QEventLoop should pass qtcore up to it's parent
(which will be different depending on platform) and then IOCP Loop or
Selector loop class should set it's own _qtcore? then QEventLoop inherits
that _qtcore.

That feels like *more* spaghetti, not less. Maybe if there was a shared
baseclass, but that would mean adding a new class for one protected
variable.

OTOH it does violate some OOP principle (and laws of nature) that a parent
can't inherit properties from it's children.

Anyway after some consideration I've made those changes.

I don't think there was any particular reason behind it, other than maybe to encourage people to use Decimals.  Given boto2 already accepted multiple python types for `N`, we already can't round trip the request, i.e `decode(encode(original_type)) != original_type`.

I think the ideal behavior would be to round trip the original customer's type.  If we could definitively tell that the original item came was an integer, then I would be ok with this.  However, given `{"N": "1"}`, we don't know if it came an integer or a decimal.  I would actually suggest in our docs that we try to encourage Decimals as the preferred type as they will actually round trip.

The purpose of this short-circuit execution, as far as I can tell, is to ensure that `generate_request_header` can build the pre-emptive auth as needed by calling this with a `None` response. However, you've accidentally (presumably as part of a refactor) left a boolean kwarg in `generate_request_header` that is unused.

I wonder if, rather than put the short-circuit all the way down here (in an arguably unrelated bit of code), the higher level function (`generate_request_header`) shouldn't just skip the the call to this function based on the knowledge it has. That would avoid some overhead (a function call and possibly some regex building), and also compartmentalises the logic a lot better.

It's actually orthogonal to the kwarg (you can still signal this with a `None` response), but it seems like that approach would be better. Do you disagree?

Here is the "obvious" / documented / expected way:

```python
        batch_txn = self._db.batch_snapshot(read_timestamp=committed)
        for batch in batch_txn.generate_query_batches(sql):
            do_something_with_rows( batch_txn.process(batch))  # might  be on another host
        batch_txn.close()
```

`Database.generate_query_batches` is a convenience wrapper:  it calls the underling `Snapshot.partition_query` method and returns results suitable for serialization / processing by `Database.process()`.

Making the lower level `partition_query` private is a bit BDSM for Python:  it is the wrapper we have for the published `PartitionQueryRequest` API.  In this case, it is like the `Session.create` / `Session.delete` API wrappers:  they are low-level API wrappers used by the higher-level database convenience methods.

Updating the unit tests has me thinking that we should enforce a limit of 0.82 or 1 or we should return NaN. 

For ``disc``, the input conditions that yield ``kt > 1.25`` will produce ``dni = 0``. This because ``Kn`` becomes negative and we set negative values to 0.

For ``dirint``, the input conditions that yield ``kt_prime > 1`` will produce ``dni = NaN``.

Here's the line in ``dirint`` that currently limits ``kt_prime`` to 0.82:

https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/blob/master/pvlib/irradiance.py#L1358

These lines assign bins for ``kt_prime`` values [0, 1]:

https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/blob/master/pvlib/irradiance.py#L1381-L1387

These lines will ensure that ``kt_prime`` values > 1 will become NaN:

https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/blob/master/pvlib/irradiance.py#L1425-L1428

One could argue that returning NaN is the right thing to do because the authors did not anticipate the model being applied for clearness index > 1. Or one could argue that we should limit the clearness index to 0.82 or 1 and move on.

This alternative aerosol forcing relationship is from [Stevens 2015](http://journals.ametsoc.org/doi/abs/10.1175/JCLI-D-14-00656.1) and is not the default treatment in v1.1. In this reference natural emissions are in Mt SO2 per year. However, I have assumed that input files use a MAGICC-style spreadsheet (dangerous to assume I know) in which sulphate emissions are in Mt S per year. So, as a general improvement, I think all emissions species need to have names and units attached to them.
